parts.text: On the second he gripped her wrist and whispered the chest must reach the monastery past the northern ridge, swear it. She swore. On the third morning he was cold. She buried him near the fig tree and sat beside the iron chest until the sky turned copper. She didn't open it that night or the next. On the third night her hands moved before her mind agreed. Inside — sapphires, rubies, coins stamped with a king's face she didn't recognize. Enough to buy the entire cliff.

Maren walked home carrying the chest, heavier now not because of jewels but because she understood the weight was never in the iron, it lived in the wanting. She reached the crossroads where the northern path split from the road home. One led to the monastery. The other curved toward her village, toward warmth, toward a life finally soft. She stood perfectly still.
